Name: task-aware_pruning
Title: Task-Aware Information-Theoretic Model Pruning: Leveraging Mutual Information for Optimal Compression
Short Hypothesis: This proposal hypothesizes that a task-aware pruning strategy, guided by mutual information between model parameters and task performance, can improve model compression efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
Related Work: While recent works have explored model pruning through rate-distortion theory, they often lack a direct correlation between task relevance and compression effectiveness. For instance, 'Successive Pruning for Model Compression via Rate Distortion Theory' primarily discusses theoretical limits without practical applications. This proposal distinguishes itself by integrating mutual information metrics to identify the most task-relevant weights to prune.
Abstract: Neural network compression often relies on heuristics that do not adequately reflect the task-specific importance of model parameters. This paper proposes a novel task-aware pruning strategy grounded in information theory, specifically leveraging mutual information to quantify the relevance of individual weights to task performance. By framing pruning as an optimization problem that minimizes task-relevant distortion, we aim to derive bounds on the achievable compression rates while maintaining model accuracy. The proposed method will be evaluated on standard benchmarks, assessing both compression efficiency and predictive performance against traditional pruning strategies. Through this work, we aim to bridge the gap between theoretical insights from information theory and practical applications in model compression, providing a clear pathway towards more efficient neural architectures that retain their task-oriented capabilities.
Experiments: 1. Mutual Information Estimation: Estimate mutual information between model weights and task outcomes using InfoNCE-style methods. 2. Pruning Strategy Implementation: Implement a pruning algorithm that removes weights based on their estimated mutual information, ranking weights from least to most relevant. 3. Evaluation Metrics: Measure compression rates (bits per weight), predictive accuracy (e.g., top-1 accuracy on classification tasks), and model size reduction. 4. Comparison Benchmarks: Compare against standard pruning methods (e.g., magnitude-based pruning, random pruning) and evaluate on several datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Estimating mutual information accurately can be computationally intensive and may introduce bias if the underlying assumptions do not hold. The pruning method may not generalize well across different architectures or tasks, necessitating further refinement and testing. There may be challenges in balancing the trade-off between compression and model accuracy, particularly in highly complex models.
